title: Understanding and Sharing Personal Stories
simple_explanation: This page helps us dig deeper into stories. We learn that authors sometimes tell us how they felt about an event (like a mini-lesson!). We also practice taking someone's exact words and changing them into our own words to tell the story. And remember, choosing the perfect word makes your writing shine!
detailed_explanation: When you answer questions about a text, it's really helpful to find 'short quotations'. These are short snippets of the author's own words that prove your answer. Sometimes you need to change 'direct speech' (exact words spoken) into 'reported speech' (telling someone *what* was said). When you do this, you might need to change verbs to a different past tense and pronouns (like 'I' to 'she'). Good writers use 'precise verbs' and 'adverbial phrases' to make their descriptions super clear and exciting. Precise verbs tell you exactly how someone did something, and adverbial phrases tell you when or where.

examples_expanded.text: Finding a quotation: If asked how Jane was amazed, you might quote, 'I could hardly believe it!'
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of biography and autobiography analysis in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: Changing direct to reported speech: If Jane said, 'I began to fear the chimp would not come,' in reported speech it becomes, 'Jane said that she had begun to fear the chimp would not come.'
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of biography and autobiography analysis in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: Precise verbs and adverbial phrases: Instead of 'She walked quickly', you could say 'She sprinted urgently' or 'She dashed away immediately'.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of biography and autobiography analysis in a real sentence.

guided_practice: Identify the meaning of 'undergrowth' in a sentence. Remember it means: Bushes and long grasses in a forest.
Match the word 'dense' with a similar concept from your own experience.
Look at the first example again. How would you modify it to make it your own?
application_tasks: Create 3 of your own sentences using the core idea of Biography and Autobiography Analysis.
Imagine you are teaching this topic to a friend. Write down exactly what you would say.
challenge_tasks: Combine the concept of 'undergrowth' with something you learned in a previous chapter to write a short paragraph.
Can you find an exception to this rule or a creative way to bend it? Explain how.
key_points: Reflective comments show the author's thoughts and learning from events.
Use short quotations to support your answers about a text.
Transforming direct speech to reported speech often requires changing verbs and pronouns.
Use precise verbs and adverbial phrases to make your writing clear and impactful.

vocabulary.definition: Bushes and long grasses in a forest.
Enjoyed eating a large amount of food.
A thought or opinion shared by the writer, looking back at an event.
The exact words someone says, usually in quotation marks.
Telling someone what was said, without using the exact words.
Verbs like 'can', 'could', 'will', 'would', 'may', 'might', 'must', 'shall', 'should' that express possibility or necessity.
Words that take the place of nouns, like 'I', 'you', 'he', 'she', 'it', 'we', 'they'.
Verbs that describe an action very exactly (e.g., 'sprinted' instead of 'ran').
